steps.voice: This is the main dashboard — RPM across the top, speed in the centre, eight data panels on the sides. Everything else lives one tap away.
A short tap anywhere on the screen reveals the Menu button in the top-right corner for six seconds.
There it is — the blue pill at the top right. Tap it to open the menu.
This is the Setup Menu — three things live here.
Layout: pick which dashboard is active. You can save multiple — a Street layout, a Track layout, a diagnostics view — and swap between them in one tap.
Splash: change the boot-up logo. Upload your own through the web editor later.
And Device Settings — where everything else is configured. Let's go in.
Device Settings. Eight sections, scrollable. I'll walk through each one.
CAN BUS — pick the bitrate your ECU broadcasts at, and which ECU preset to use. This is what gets your signals mapping correctly.
Device Info — your dash's serial number, firmware version, and MAC address. Handy when contacting support.
Scrolling down. Network & Updates — join Wi-Fi, start the hotspot, show a QR for your phone to connect, or check for firmware updates over the air.

Display — live brightness slider, a dimmer-switch binding so your headlights can dim the dash automatically, night-mode toggle, and screen rotation. The slider actually dims the canvas in the sandbox — try it after this tour.
Data Logging — start or stop a log to the SD card and pick the sample rate, one hertz up to two hundred.
Peak Hold — view the all-time maximum and minimum of every signal. Useful for after a dyno pull or a lap. Reset Peaks wipes the list.
Testing — flips the signal simulator so you can see the dashboard animated without real CAN traffic. Perfect for previewing a new layout on the bench.

CAN Bus diagnostics — live health dot, RX frame rate, and an expandable details grid with error counters. The Run Bus Scan button sweeps the four common bitrates looking for traffic, same as first-boot.
Finally — System Diagnostics opens a read-only health dashboard. Run Setup Wizard reruns the first-boot flow. Reset to Default factory-wipes everything — your layouts, your Wi-Fi, the lot.
Close returns to the dashboard. Everything you changed persists in NVS, so the next power-up picks up where you left off.
And we're home. That's Device Settings.
